lessons.title: Identifying Common Prompt Issues
lessons.concept_snapshot: Common prompt issues in AI include ambiguity, lack of context, overly complex or vague instructions, and incorrect assumptions about AI capabilities. These issues can lead to inaccurate or irrelevant outputs, especially in complex domains like FinTech. Identifying these problems requires understanding both the AI model's limitations and the specific domain requirements. Effective prompt debugging involves iterating on prompt design to clarify intent and provide sufficient context.

lessons.title: Introduction to Tokens in Language Models
lessons.concept_snapshot: Tokens are the fundamental units of text that language models process. They can be words, subwords, or characters, depending on the model's design. In language models, text is broken down into tokens, which are then used to predict the next token in a sequence. Understanding tokens is crucial for optimizing language model performance, particularly in contexts like financial data analysis where precision is key.

lessons.title: Understanding Data Leakage in AI Systems
lessons.concept_snapshot: Data leakage in AI systems occurs when information from outside the training dataset inadvertently influences the model, leading to overly optimistic performance metrics. Common sources include test data inadvertently used in training, features derived from the target variable, and time-based data splits that don't respect temporal order. In healthcare, data leakage can lead to models that seem accurate but fail in real-world settings, potentially compromising patient safety. Identifying and mitigating data leakage is crucial for building robust, reliable AI models in clinical analytics.

Caching stores frequently accessed data in memory to reduce retrieval times, while batching groups data into chunks for processing to minimize overhead. In retail data pipelines, these techniques can significantly speed up operations like inventory analysis and sales reporting. Implementing them effectively requires understanding the data flow and identifying key points where caching and batching provide the most benefit.

lessons.title: Understanding Embeddings in AI
lessons.concept_snapshot: Embeddings are numerical representations of data that capture semantic meaning, enabling AI systems to understand relationships between items like products or customer reviews. In retail, embeddings can be used to enhance recommendation systems by identifying similar products or customer preferences. They transform complex data into a format that AI models can process efficiently, facilitating tasks like clustering and similarity search. Understanding embeddings is crucial for AI data engineers to build robust, intelligent data pipelines and analytics solutions.

lessons.title: Understanding Vector and Keyword Retrieval
lessons.concept_snapshot: Vector retrieval uses mathematical representations of data to find similar items, ideal for unstructured data like images or text. Keyword retrieval relies on exact matches of terms, suitable for structured data like databases. Hybrid retrieval combines both methods to leverage the strengths of each, enhancing search accuracy and relevance. In retail, this approach can optimize product search by understanding both customer queries and product descriptions.

lessons.title: Introduction to Tokens in LLMs
lessons.concept_snapshot: Tokens are the fundamental units of text that large language models (LLMs) process. In LLMs, text is broken down into tokens, which can be words, subwords, or even characters, depending on the model. Tokens are crucial because they determine how the model interprets and generates language. Understanding tokens helps in optimizing model performance and managing context length, especially in security-sensitive applications like banking.

lessons.title: Assessment: LLM Fundamentals
lessons.concept_snapshot: Large Language Models (LLMs) process text by breaking it into tokens, understanding context, and predicting the next token in a sequence. Tokens are the basic units of text, such as words or subwords, that LLMs use to interpret input. Context refers to the surrounding text that influences how the model understands and predicts tokens. Prediction is the model's ability to generate the next token based on the given context, which is crucial for tasks like text generation and completion.

lessons.title: Introduction to Data Minimization
lessons.concept_snapshot: Data minimization in banking involves collecting only the necessary data needed for specific purposes, reducing the risk of data breaches and ensuring compliance with regulations. It is a critical principle in data privacy, particularly in an industry handling sensitive financial information. Effective data minimization requires understanding the data lifecycle and identifying what data is essential. This practice not only protects customer privacy but also enhances data security and operational efficiency.

lessons.title: Understanding Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
lessons.concept_snapshot: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a security vulnerability that allows attackers to inject malicious scripts into web applications, potentially compromising user data and application integrity. In the context of AI outputs, XSS can occur when AI-generated content is displayed in web interfaces without proper sanitization. This is particularly critical in banking, where sensitive financial data may be exposed. Understanding and mitigating XSS in AI systems is essential for maintaining secure and trustworthy applications.
